perturbation.original_text: Bizzingo agrees that any use of the Property for advertising, promotional or sale purposes will be approved in advance by Theismann. Theismann agrees that such material, submitted for approval as provided herein may be deemed by Bizzingo to have been approved hereunder if the same is not disapproved by the Theismann in writing within fourteen (14) days after Theismann's receipt thereof. Theismann agrees that any material submitted hereunder will not be unreasonably disapproved and, if it is disapproved, that Theismann shall advise Bizzingo in writing of the specific grounds therefor at the time of disapproval and provide Bizzingo with specific changes or alterations to such materials.
perturbation.changed_text: Bizzingo may use the Property for advertising, promotional or sale purposes. Theismann agrees that such use shall be at Bizzingo's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the requirement for Theismann's prior approval for advertising and promotional materials. This creates a legal risk if the advertising is false or misleading since Theismann's likeness is being used.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 43(a) - False designations of origin, false descriptions, and dilution prohibited
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification potentially contradicts 15 U.S.C. § 43(a) because it removes the safeguard of Theismann's approval, which could allow Bizzingo to use his endorsement in a way that is false or misleading, thereby damaging his reputation and potentially misleading consumers. By eliminating Theismann's approval, the contract enables Bizzingo to create advertisements that misrepresent the product or Theismann's association with it, violating the federal law against false advertising.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 2 / SUBCHAPTER I / § 43 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 43 : Office and place of meeting Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 2-FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION; PROMOTION OF EXPORT TRADE AND PREVENTION OF UNFAIR METHODS OF COMPETITION SUBCHAPTER I-FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous §43. Office and place of meeting The principal office of the Commission shall be in the city of Washington, but it may meet and exercise all its powers at any other place. The Commission may, by one or more of its members, or by such examiners as it may designate, prosecute any inquiry necessary to its duties in any part of the United States. ( Sept. 26, 1914, ch. 311,
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates Theismann's approval for Bizzingo's use of the property for advertising. The changed contract eliminates this requirement, granting Bizzingo sole discretion. The Law Explanation suggests this creates a risk of false or misleading advertising, potentially violating 15 U.S.C. § 43(a), as it removes Theismann's safeguard. The provided snippet for 15 USC 43 pertains to the location of the Federal Trade Commission's office and meetings, not false advertising. However, given the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, the removal of Theismann's approval process creates a significant risk that Bizzingo could use Theismann's likeness in misleading ways, which is the basis of the legal risk of false advertising.

perturbation.original_text: Bizzingo agrees to defend, indemnify, and hold Theismann harmless against all costs, expenses, and losses (including reasonable attorney fees and costs) incurred through claims of third parties against Theismann based on the manufacture or sale of the Network including, but not limited to, actions founded on network liability. In this regarding, within thirty (30) days from the execution of this Agreement, Bizzingo will secure an insurance policy with limits of $5,000,000 per event and $ 5,000,000 umbrella, naming Theismann as an additional insured, covering the losses and claims stated in this sub-paragraph d.
perturbation.changed_text: Bizzingo agrees to defend, indemnify, and hold Theismann harmless against all costs, expenses, and losses (including reasonable attorney fees and costs) incurred through claims of third parties against Theismann based on the manufacture or sale of the Network including, but not limited to, actions founded on network liability.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for Bizzingo to secure an insurance policy naming Theismann as an additional insured, the contract exposes Theismann to significant financial risk. This omission contradicts standard business practices and could violate implied duties of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Common Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: The removal of the insurance requirement contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which is a common law principle requiring parties to act honestly and reasonably in the performance of their contractual obligations. By not securing insurance, Bizzingo is failing to take reasonable steps to protect Theismann from potential liabilities arising from the product endorsement, effectively shifting all the risk onto Theismann without his explicit consent, thereby violating the spirit of the agreement and the implied duty of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The omission of the insurance policy requirement directly contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as encompassing "honest dealing" and "faithful performance of duties." By removing the insurance requirement, Bizzingo fails to protect Theismann from potential liabilities, which could be interpreted as a failure to act honestly and reasonably, thus creating a legal contradiction in the form of an omission that violates good faith. The original text ensures a risk mitigation measure (insurance), whereas the changed text removes this protection, shifting the financial risk entirely to Theismann.

perturbation.original_text: Theismann or his representatives, at his cost and expense, shall have the right, upon reasonable notice and during normal business hours, to inspect Bizzingo's books and records and all other documents and material in Bizzingo's possession or control with respect to the determination of Royalties payable hereunder. Theismann shall have free and full access thereto for such purposes and may make copies thereof. All books and records relative to Bizzingo's obligations hereunder shall be maintained and made accessible to Theismann for inspection at a location in the United States for at least one year after termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Theismann or his representatives, at his cost and expense, may request to inspect Bizzingo's books and records with respect to the determination of Royalties payable hereunder. All decisions regarding access shall be at Bizzingo's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the guaranteed right of inspection and places it entirely at Bizzingo's discretion, making it difficult for Theismann to verify the accuracy of royalty payments. This could be seen as a violation of good faith and fair dealing, especially if Bizzingo withholds information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Common Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: By making access to financial records entirely discretionary, Bizzingo undermines Theismann's ability to ensure he is being paid correctly. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing because it allows Bizzingo to potentially conceal information or manipulate royalty calculations without Theismann having a reasonable way to verify them. Such actions would be considered a breach of the implicit understanding that both parties will act honestly and reasonably in fulfilling their contractual obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes Theismann's guaranteed right to inspect Bizzingo's books and records, placing access at Bizzingo's sole discretion. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which, according to Scraped Law Snippet 1, requires "observance of fair dealing standards". By potentially concealing information or manipulating royalty calculations without Theismann having a reasonable way to verify them, Bizzingo fails to act in good faith.
